text: There was a man in the land of Hus, whose name was Job, and that man was simple and upright, and fearing God, and avoiding evil.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And there were born to him seven sons and three daughters.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And his possession was seven thousand sheep, and three thousand camels, and five hundred yoke of oxen, and five hundred she asses, and a family exceedingly great: and this man was great among all the people of the east.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And his sons went, and made a feast by houses, every one in his day. And sending, they called their three sisters, to eat and drink with them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when the days of their feasting were gone about, Job sent to them, and sanctified them: and rising up early, offered holocausts for every one of them. For he said: Lest perhaps my sons have sinned, and have blessed God in their hearts. So did Job all days.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Now on a certain day, when the sons of God came to stand before the Lord, Satan also was present among them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the Lord said to him: Whence comest thou? And he answered and said: I have gone round about the earth, and walked through it.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the Lord said to him: Hast thou considered my servant, Job, that there is none like him in the earth, a simple and upright man, and fearing God, and avoiding evil?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Satan answering, said: Doth Job fear God in vain?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Hast thou not made a fence for him, and his house, and all his substance round about, blessed the works of his hands, and his possession hath increased on the earth?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But stretch forth thy hand a little, and touch all that he hath, and see if he bless thee not to thy face.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then the Lord said to Satan: Behold, all that he hath is in thy hand: only put not forth thy hand upon his person. And Satan went forth from the presence of the Lord.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Now upon a certain day, when his sons and daughters were eating and drinking wine, in the house of their eldest brother,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: There came a messenger to Job, and said: The oxen were ploughing, and the asses feeding beside them,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the Sabeans rushed in, and took all away, and slew the servants with the sword; and I alone have escaped to tell thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And while he was yet speaking, another came, and said: The fire of God fell from heaven, and striking the sheep and the servants, hath consumed them; and I alone have escaped to tell thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And while he also was yet speaking, there came another, and said: The Chaldeans made three troops, and have fallen upon the camels, and taken them; moreover, they have slain the servants with the sword: and I alone have escaped to tell thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He was yet speaking, and behold another came in, and said: Thy sons and daughters were eating and drinking wine in the house of their eldest brother,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A violent wind came on a sudden from the side of the desert, and shook the four corners of the house, and it fell upon thy children, and they are dead: and I alone have escaped to tell thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then Job rose up, and rent his garments, and having shaven his head, fell down upon the ground, and worshipped,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And said: Naked came I out of my mother’s womb, and naked shall I return thither: the Lord gave, and the Lord hath taken away: as it hath pleased the Lord, so is it done: blessed be the name of the Lord.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: In all these things Job sinned not by his lips, nor spoke he any foolish thing against God.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And it came to pass, when on a certain day the sons of God came, and stood before the Lord, and Satan came amongst them, and stood in his sight,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: That the Lord said to Satan: Whence comest thou? And he answered, and said: I have gone round about the earth, and walked through it.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the Lord said to Satan: Hast thou considered my servant, Job, that there is none like him in the earth, a man simple and upright, and fearing God, and avoiding evil, and still keeping his innocence? But thou hast moved me against him, that I should afflict him without cause.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Satan answered, and said: Skin for skin; and all that a man hath, he will give for his life:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But put forth thy hand, and touch his bone and his flesh, and then thou shalt see that he will bless thee to thy face.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the Lord said to Satan: Behold, he is in thy hand, but yet save his life.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So Satan went forth from the presence of the Lord, and struck Job with a very grievous ulcer, from the sole of the foot even to the top of his head:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he took a potsherd and scraped the corrupt matter, sitting on a dunghill.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And his wife said to him: Dost thou still continue in thy simplicity? bless God and die.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he said to her: Thou hast spoken like one of the foolish women: If we have received good things at the hand of God, why should we not receive evil? In all these things Job did not sin with his lips.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Now when Job’s three friends heard all the evil that had befallen him, they came every one from his own place, Eliphaz, the Themanite, and Baldad, the Suhite, and Sophar, the Naamathite. For they had made an appointment to come together and visit him, and comfort him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when they had lifted up their eyes afar off, they knew him not, and crying out, they wept, and rending their garments, they sprinkled dust upon their heads toward heaven.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they sat with him on the ground seven days and seven nights and no man spoke to him a word: for they saw that his grief was very great.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: After this, Job opened his mouth, and cursed his day,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he said:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Let the day perish wherein I was born, and the night in which it was said: A man child is conceived.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Let that day be turned into darkness, let not God regard it from above, and let not the light shine upon it.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Let darkness, and the shadow of death, cover it, let a mist overspread it, and let it be wrapped up in bitterness.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Let a darksome whirlwind seize upon that night, let it not be counted in the days of the year, nor numbered in the months.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Let that night be solitary, and not worthy of praise.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Let them curse it who curse the day, who are ready to raise up a leviathan:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Let the stars be darkened with the mist thereof: let it expect light, and not see it, nor the rising of the dawning of the day:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Because it shut not up the doors of the womb that bore me, nor took away evils from my eyes.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Why did I not die in the womb? why did I not perish when I came out of the belly?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Why received upon the knees? why suckled at the breasts?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For now I should have been asleep and still, and should have rest in my sleep:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: With kings and consuls of the earth, who build themselves solitudes:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Or with princes, that possess gold, and fill their houses with silver:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Or as a hidden untimely birth, I should not be; or as they that, being conceived, have not seen the light.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: There the wicked cease from tumult, and there the wearied in strength are at rest.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they sometime bound together without disquiet, have not heard the voice of the oppressor.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The small and great are there, and the servant is free from his master.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Why is light given to him that is in misery, and life to them that are in bitterness of soul?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: That look for death, and it cometh not, as they that dig for a treasure:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they rejoice exceedingly when they have found the grave?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: To a man whose way is hidden, and God hath surrounded him with darkness?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Before I eat I sigh: and as overflowing waters, so is my roaring:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For the fear which I feared, hath come upon me: and that which I was afraid of, hath befallen me.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Have I not dissembled? have I not kept silence? have I not been quiet? and indignation is come upon me.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then Eliphaz, the Themanite, answered, and said:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: If we begin to speak to thee, perhaps thou wilt take it ill; but who can withhold the words he hath conceived?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Behold thou hast taught many, and thou hast strengthened the weary hands:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Thy words have confirmed them that were staggering, and thou hast strengthened the trembling knees:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But now the scourge is come upon thee, and thou faintest: It hath touched thee, and thou art troubled.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Where is thy fear, thy fortitude, thy patience, and the perfection of thy ways?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Remember, I pray thee, who ever perished being innocent? or when were the just destroyed?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: On the contrary, I have seen those who work iniquity, and sow sorrows, and reap them,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Perishing by the blast of God, and consumed by the spirit of his wrath.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The roaring of the lion, and the voice of the lioness, and the teeth of the whelps of lions, are broken:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The tiger hath perished for want of prey, and the young lions are scattered abroad.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Now there was a word spoken to me in private, and my ears by stealth, as it were, received the veins of its whisper.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: In the horror of a vision by night, when deep sleep is wont to hold men,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Fear seized upon me, and trembling, and all my bones were affrighted:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when a spirit passed before me, the hair of my flesh stood up.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: There stood one whose countenance I knew not, an image before my eyes, and I heard the voice, as it were, of a gentle wind.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Shall man be justified in comparison of God, or shall a man be more pure than his maker?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Behold, they that serve him are not steadfast, and in his angels he found wickedness:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: How much more shall they that dwell in houses of clay, who have an earthly foundation, be consumed as with the moth?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: From morning till evening they shall be cut down: and because no one understandeth, they shall perish for ever.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they that shall be left, shall be taken away from them: they shall die, and not in wisdom.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Call now, if there be any that will answer thee, and turn to some of the saints.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Anger indeed killeth the foolish, and envy slayeth the little one.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I have seen a fool with a strong root, and I cursed his beauty immediately.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: His children shall be far from safety, and shall be destroyed in the gate, and there shall be none to deliver them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Whose harvest the hungry shall eat, and the armed man shall take him by violence, and the thirsty shall drink up his riches.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Nothing upon earth is done without a cause, and sorrow doth not spring out of the ground.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Man is born to labour, and the bird to fly.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Wherefore I will pray to the Lord, and address my speech to God:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Who doth great things, and unsearchable and wonderful things without number:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Who giveth rain upon the face of the earth, and watereth all things with waters:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Who setteth up the humble on high, and comforteth with health those that mourn.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Who bringeth to nought the designs of the malignant, so that their hands cannot accomplish what they had begun:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Who catcheth the wise in their craftiness, and disappointeth the counsel of the wicked:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: They shall meet with darkness in the day, and grope at noonday as in the night.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But he shall save the needy from the sword of their mouth, and the poor from the hand of the violent.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And to the needy there shall be hope, but iniquity shall draw in her mouth.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Blessed is the man whom God correcteth: refuse not, therefore, the chastising of the Lord.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For he woundeth, and cureth: he striketh, and his hands shall heal.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: In six troubles he shall deliver thee, and in the seventh, evil shall not touch thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: In famine he shall deliver thee from death; and in battle, from the hand of the sword.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Thou shalt be hidden from the scourge of the tongue: and thou shalt not fear calamity when it cometh.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: In destruction and famine thou shalt laugh: and thou shalt not be afraid of the beasts of the earth.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But thou shalt have a covenant with the stones of the lands, and the beasts of the earth shall be at peace with thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And thou shalt know that thy tabernacle is in peace, and visiting thy beauty, thou shalt not sin.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Thou shalt know also that thy seed shall be multiplied, and thy offspring like the grass of the earth.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Thou shalt enter into the grave in abundance, as a heap of wheat is brought in its season.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Behold, this is even so, as we have searched out: which thou having heard, consider it thoroughly in thy mind.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But Job answered, and said:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: O that my sins, whereby I have deserved wrath, and the calamity that I suffer, were weighed in a balance.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: As the sand of the sea, this would appear heavier: therefore, my words are full of sorrow:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For the arrows of the Lord are in me, the rage whereof drinketh up my spirit, and the terrors of the Lord war against me.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Will the wild ass bray when he hath grass? or will the ox low when he standeth before a full manger?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Or can an unsavoury thing be eaten, that is not seasoned with salt? or can a man taste that which, when tasted, bringeth death?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The things which before my soul would not touch, now, through anguish, are my meats.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Who will grant that my request may come: and that God may give me what I look for?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And that he that hath begun may destroy me, that he may let loose his hand, and cut me off?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And that this may be my comfort, that afflicting me with sorrow, he spare not, nor I contradict the words of the Holy one.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For what is my strength, that I can hold out? or what is my end, that I should keep patience?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: My strength is not the strength of stones, nor is my flesh of brass.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Behold there is no help for me in myself, and my familiar friends also are departed from me.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that taketh away mercy from his friend, forsaketh the fear of the Lord.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: My brethren have passed by me, as the torrent that passeth swiftly in the valleys.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: They that fear the hoary frost, the snow shall fall upon them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: At the time when they shall be scattered they shall perish: and after it groweth hot, they shall be melted out of their place.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The paths of their steps are entangled: they shall walk in vain, and shall perish.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Consider the paths of Thema, the ways of Saba, and wait a little while.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: They are confounded, because I have hoped: they are come also even unto me, and are covered with shame.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Now you are come: and now, seeing my affliction, you are afraid.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Did I say: Bring to me, and give me of your substance?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Or deliver me from the hand of the enemy, and rescue me out of the hand of the mighty?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Teach me, and I will hold my peace: and if I have been ignorant of any thing, instruct me.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Why have you detracted the words of truth, whereas there is none of you that can reprove me?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: You dress up speeches only to rebuke, and you utter words to the wind.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: You rush in upon the fatherless, and you endeavour to overthrow your friend.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: However, finish what you have begun: give ear and see whether I lie.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Answer, I beseech you, without contention: and speaking that which is just, judge ye.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And you shall not find iniquity in my tongue, neither shall folly sound in my mouth.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The life of man upon earth is a warfare, and his days are like the days of a hireling.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: As a servant longeth for the shade, as the hireling looketh for the end of his work;

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So I also have had empty months, and have numbered to myself wearisome nights.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: If I lie down to sleep, I shall say: When shall I rise? and again, I shall look for the evening, and shall be filled with sorrows even till darkness.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: My flesh is clothed with rottenness and the filth of dust; my skin is withered and drawn together.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: My days have passed more swiftly than the web is cut by the weaver, and are consumed without any hope.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Remember that my life is but wind, and my eye shall not return to see good things.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Nor shall the sight of man behold me: thy eyes are upon me, and I shall be no more.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: As a cloud is consumed, and passeth away: so he that shall go down to hell shall not come up.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Nor shall he return any more into his house, neither shall his place know him any more.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Wherefore, I will not spare my month, I will speak in the affliction of my spirit: I will talk with the bitterness of my soul.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Am I a sea, or a whale, that thou hast enclosed me in a prison?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: If I say: My bed shall comfort me, and I shall be relieved, speaking with myself on my couch:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Thou wilt frighten me with dreams, and terrify me with visions.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So that my soul rather chooseth hanging, and my bones death.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I have done with hope, I shall now live no longer: spare me, for my days are nothing.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: What is a man, that thou shouldst magnify him or why dost thou set thy heart upon him?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Thou visitest him early in the morning, and thou provest him suddenly.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: How long wilt thou not spare me, nor suffer me to swallow down my spittle?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I have sinned: what shall I do to thee, O keeper of men? why hast thou set me opposite to thee, and am I become burdensome to myself?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Why dost thou not remove my sin, and why dost thou not take away my iniquity? Behold now I shall sleep in the dust: and if thou seek me in the morning, I shall not be.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then Baldad, the Suhite, answered, and said:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: How long wilt thou speak these things, and how long shall the words of thy mouth be like a strong wind?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Doth God pervert judgment, or doth the Almighty overthrow that which is just?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Although thy children have sinned against him, and he hath left them in the hand of their iniquity:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Yet if thou wilt arise early to God, and wilt beseech the Almighty:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: If thou wilt walk clean and upright, he will presently awake unto thee, and will make the dwelling of thy justice peaceable:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: In so much, that if thy former things were small thy latter things would be multiplied exceedingly.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For inquire of the former generation, and search diligently into the memory of the fathers:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: (For we are but of yesterday, and are ignorant that our days upon earth are but a shadow.)

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they shall teach thee: they shall speak to thee, and utter words out of their hearts.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Can the rush be green without moisture? or sedge bush grow without water?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: When it is yet in flower, and is not plucked up with the hand, it withereth before all herbs.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Even so are the ways of all that forget God, and the hope of the hypocrite shall perish:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: His folly shall not please him, and his trust shall be like the spider’s web.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He shall lean upon his house, and it shall not stand: he shall prop it up, and it shall not rise:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He seemeth to have moisture before the sun cometh; and at his rising, his blossom shall shoot forth.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: His roots shall be thick upon a heap of stones; and among the stones he shall abide.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: If one swallow him up out of his place, he shall deny him, and shall say: I know thee not.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For this is the joy of his way, that others may spring again out of the earth.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: God will not cast away the simple, nor reach out his hand to the evil doer:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Until thy mouth be filled with laughter, and thy lips with rejoicing.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: They that hate thee, shall be clothed with confusion: and the dwelling of the wicked shall not stand.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Job answered, and said:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Indeed I know it is so, and that man cannot be justified, compared with God.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: If he will contend with him, he cannot answer him one for a thousand.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He is wise in heart, and mighty in strength: who hath resisted him, and hath had peace?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Who hath removed mountains, and they whom he overthrew in his wrath, knew it not.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Who shaketh the earth out of her place, and the pillars thereof tremble.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Who commandeth the sun, and it riseth not: and shutteth up the stars, as it were, under a seal:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Who alone spreadeth out the heavens, and walketh upon the waves of the sea,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Who maketh Arcturus, and Orion, and Hyades, and the inner parts of the south.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Who doth things great and incomprehensible, and wonderful, of which there is no number.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: If he come to me, I shall not see him: if he depart, I shall not understand.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: If he examine on a sudden, who shall answer him? or who can say: Why dost thou so?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: God, whose wrath no man can resist, and under whom they stoop that bear up the world.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: What am I then, that I should answer him, and have words with him?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I, who although I should have any just thing, would not answer, but would make supplication to my judge.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And if he should hear me when I call, I should not believe that he had heard my voice.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For he shall crush me in a whirlwind, and multiply my wounds even without cause.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He alloweth not my spirit to rest, and he filleth me with bitterness.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: If strength be demanded, he is most strong: if equity of judgment, no man dare bear witness for me.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: If I would justify myself, my own mouth shall condemn me: if I would shew myself innocent, he shall prove me wicked.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Although I should be simple, even this my soul shall be ignorant of, and I shall be weary of my life.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: One thing there is that I have spoken, both the innocent and the wicked he consumeth.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: If he scourge, let him kill at once, and not laugh at the pains of the innocent.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The earth is given into the hand of the wicked, he covereth the face of the judges thereof: and if it be not he, who is it then?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: My days have been swifter than a post: they have fled away and have not seen good.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: They have passed by as ships carrying fruits, as an eagle flying to the prey.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: If I say: I will not speak so: I change my face, and am tormented with sorrow.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I feared all my works, knowing that thou didst not spare the offender.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But if so also I am wicked, why have I laboured in vain?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: If I be washed, as it were, with snow waters, and my hands shall shine ever so clean:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Yet thou shalt plunge me in filth, and my garments shall abhor me.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For I shall not answer a man that is like myself: nor one that may be heard with me equally in judgment.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: There is none that may be able to reprove both, and to put his hand between both.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Let him take his rod away from me, and let not his fear terrify me.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I will speak, and will not fear him: for I cannot answer while I am in fear.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: My soul is weary of my life, I will let go my speech against myself, I will speak in the bitterness of my soul.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I will say to God: Do not condemn me: tell me why thou judgest me so?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Doth it seem good to thee that thou shouldst calumniate me, and oppress me, the work of thy own hands, and help the counsel of the wicked?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Hast thou eyes of flesh: or, shalt thou see as man seeth?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Are thy days as the days of man, and are thy years as the times of men:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: That thou shouldst inquire after my iniquity, and search after my sin?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And shouldst know that I have done no wicked thing, whereas there is no man that can deliver out of thy hand?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Thy hands have made me, and fashioned me wholly round about, and dost thou thus cast me down headlong on a sudden?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Remember, I beseech thee, that thou hast made me as the clay, and thou wilt bring me into dust.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Hast thou not milked me as milk, and curdled me like cheese?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Thou hast clothed me with skin and flesh: thou hast put me together with bones and sinews:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Thou hast granted me life and mercy, and thy visitation hath preserved my spirit.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Although thou conceal these things in thy heart, yet I know that thou rememberest all things.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: If I have sinned, and thou hast spared me for an hour: why dost thou not suffer me to be clean from my iniquity?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And if I be wicked, woe unto me: and if just, I shall not lift up my head, being filled with affliction and misery.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And for pride thou wilt take me as a lioness, and returning, thou tormentest me wonderfully.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Thou renewest thy witnesses against me, and multipliest thy wrath upon me, and pains war against me.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Why didst thou bring me forth out of the womb? O that I had been consumed, that eye might not see me!

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I should have been as if I had not been, carried from the womb to the grave.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Shall not the fewness of my days be ended shortly? Suffer me, therefore, that I may lament my sorrow a little:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Before I go and return no more, to a land that is dark and covered with the mist of death:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A land of misery and darkness, where the shadow of death, and no order, but everlasting horror dwelleth.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then Sophar the Naamathite answered, and said:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Shall not he that speaketh much, hear also? or shall a man full of talk be justified?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Shall men hold their peace to thee only? and when thou hast mocked others, shall no man confute thee?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For thou hast said: My word is pure, and I am clean in thy sight.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And I wish that God would speak with thee, and would open his lips to thee,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: That he might shew thee the secrets of wisdom, and that his law is manifold, and thou mightest understand that he exacteth much less of thee, than thy iniquity deserveth.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Peradventure thou wilt comprehend the steps of God, and wilt find out the Almighty perfectly?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He is higher than heaven, and what wilt thou do? he is deeper than hell, and how wilt thou know?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The measure of him is longer than the earth, and broader than the sea.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: If he shall overturn all things, or shall press them together, who shall contradict him?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For he knoweth the vanity of men, and when he seeth iniquity, doth he not consider it?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A vain man is lifted up into pride, and thinketh himself born free like a wild ass’s colt.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But thou hast hardened thy heart, and hast spread thy hands to him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: If thou wilt put away from thee the iniquity that is in thy hand, and let not injustice remain in thy tabernacle:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then mayst thou lift up thy face without spot, and thou shalt be steadfast, and shalt not fear.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Thou shalt also forget misery, and remember it only as waters that are passed away.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And brightness like that of the noonday, shall arise to thee at evening: and when thou shalt think thyself consumed, thou shalt rise as the day star.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And thou shalt have confidence, hope being set before thee, and being buried thou shalt sleep secure.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Thou shalt rest, and there shall be none to make thee afraid: and many shall entreat thy face.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But the eyes of the wicked shall decay, and the way to escape shall fail them, and their hope the abomination of the soul.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then Job answered, and said:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Are you then men alone, and shall wisdom die with you?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I also have a heart as well as you: for who is ignorant of these things, which you know?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that is mocked by his friends as I, shall call upon God and he will hear him: for the simplicity of the just man is laughed to scorn.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The lamp despised in the thoughts of the rich, is ready for the time appointed.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The tabernacles of robbers abound, and they provoke God boldly; whereas it is he that hath given all into their hands:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But ask now the beasts, and they shall teach thee: and the birds of the air, and they shall tell thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Speak to the earth, and it shall answer thee: and the fishes of the sea shall tell.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Who is ignorant that the hand of the Lord hath made all these things?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: In whose hand is the soul of every living thing, and the spirit of all flesh of man.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Doth not the ear discern words, and the palate of him that eateth, the taste?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: In the ancient is wisdom, and in length of days prudence.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: With him is wisdom and strength, he hath counsel and understanding.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: If he pull down, there is no man that can build up: if he shut up a man, there is none that can open.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: If he withhold the waters, all things shall be dried up: and if he send them out, they shall overturn the earth.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: With him is strength and wisdom: he knoweth both the deceivers, and him that is deceived.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He bringeth counsellors to a foolish end, and judges to insensibility.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He looseth the belt of kings, and girdeth their loins with a cord.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He leadeth away priests without glory, and overthroweth nobles.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He changeth the speech of the true speakers, and taketh away the doctrine of the aged.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He poureth contempt upon princes, and relieveth them that were oppressed.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He discovereth deep things out of darkness, and bringeth up to light the shadow of death.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He multiplieth nations, and destroyeth them, and restoreth them again after they were overthrown.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He changeth the heart of the princes of the people of the earth, and deceiveth them that they walk in vain where there is no way.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: They shall grope as in the dark, and not in the light, and he shall make them stagger like men that are drunk.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Behold my eye hath seen all these things, and my ear hath heard them, and I have understood them all.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: According to your knowledge I also know: neither am I inferior to you.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But yet I will speak to the Almighty, and I desire to reason with God.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Having first shewn that you are forgers of lies, and maintainers of perverse opinions.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And I wish you would hold your peace, that you might be thought to be wise men.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Hear ye therefore my reproof, and attend to the judgment of my lips.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Hath God any need of your lie, that you should speak deceitfully for him?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Do you accept this person, and do you endeavour to judge for God?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Or shall it please him, from whom nothing can be concealed? or shall he be deceived as a man, with your deceitful dealings?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He shall reprove you, because in secret you accept his person.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: As soon as he shall move himself, he shall trouble you: and his dread shall fall upon you.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Your remembrance shall be compared to ashes, and your necks shall be brought to clay.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Hold your peace a little while, that I may speak whatsoever my mind shall suggest to me.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Why do I tear my flesh with my teeth, and carry my soul in my hands?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Although he should kill me, I will trust in him: but yet I will reprove my ways in his sight.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he shall be my saviour: for no hypocrite shall come before his presence.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Hear ye my speech, and receive with your ears hidden truths.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: If I shall be judged, I know that I shall be found just.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Who is he that will plead against me? let him come: why am I consumed holding my peace?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Two things only do not to me, and then from thy face I shall not be hid:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Withdraw thy hand far from me, and let not thy dread terrify me.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Call me, and I will answer thee: or else I will speak, and do thou answer me.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: How many are my iniquities and sins? make me know my crimes and offenses.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Why hidest thou thy face, and thinkest me thy enemy?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Against a leaf, that is carried away with the wind, thou shewest thy power, and thou pursuest a dry straw.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For thou writest bitter things against me, and wilt consume me for the sins of my youth.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Thou hast put my feet in the stocks, and hast observed all my paths, and hast considered the steps of my feet:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Who am to be consumed as rottenness, and as a garment that is motheaten.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Man born of a woman, living for a short time, is filled with many miseries.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Who cometh forth like a flower, and is destroyed, and fleeth as a shadow, and never continueth in the same state.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And dost thou think it meet to open thy eyes upon such an one, and to bring him into judgment with thee?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Who can make him clean that is conceived of unclean seed? is it not thou who only art?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The days of man are short, and the number of his months is with thee: thou hast appointed his bounds which cannot be passed.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Depart a little from him, that he may rest until his wished for day come, as that of the hireling.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A tree hath hope: if it be cut, it groweth green again, and the boughs thereof sprout.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: If its roots be old in the earth, and its stock be dead in the dust:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: At the scent of water, it shall spring, and bring forth leaves, as when it was first planted.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But man when he shall be dead, and stripped and consumed, I pray you where is he?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: As if the waters should depart out of the sea, and an emptied river should be dried up;

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So man when he is fallen asleep shall not rise again; till the heavens be broken, he shall not awake, nor rise up out of his sleep.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Who will grant me this, that thou mayst protect me in hell, and hide me till thy wrath pass, and appoint me a time when thou wilt remember me?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Shall man that is dead, thinkest thou, live again? all the days in which I am now in warfare, I expect until my change come.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Thou shalt call me, and I will answer thee: to the work of thy hands thou shalt reach out thy right hand.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Thou indeed hast numbered my steps, but spare my sins.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Thou hast sealed up my offences as it were in a bag, but hast cured my iniquity.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A mountain falling cometh to nought, and a rock is removed out of its place.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Waters wear away the stones, and with inundation the ground by little and little is washed away: so in like manner thou shalt destroy man.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Thou hast strengthened him for a little while, that he may pass away for ever: thou shalt change his face, and shalt send him away.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Whether his children come to honour or dishonour, he shall not understand.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But yet his flesh, while he shall live, shall have pain, and his soul shall mourn over him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Eliphaz the Themanite, answered, and said:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Will a wise man answer as if he were speaking in the wind, and fill his stomach with burning heat?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Thou reprovest him by words, who is not equal to thee, and thou speakest that which is not good for thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: As much as is in thee, thou hast made void fear, and hast taken away prayers from before God.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For thy iniquity hath taught thy mouth, and thou imitatest the tongue of blasphemers.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Thy own mouth shall condemn thee, and not I: and thy own lips shall answer thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Art thou the first man that was born, or wast thou made before the hills?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Hast thou heard God’s counsel, and shall his wisdom be inferior to thee?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: What knowest thou that we are ignorant of? what dost thou understand that we know not?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: There are with us also aged and ancient men, much elder than thy fathers.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Is it a great matter that God should comfort thee? but thy wicked words hinder this.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Why doth thy heart elevate thee, and why dost thou stare with thy eyes, as if they were thinking great things?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Why doth thy spirit swell against God, to utter such words out of thy mouth?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: What is man that he should be without spot, and he that is born of a woman that he should appear just?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Behold among his saints none is unchangeable, and the heavens are not pure in his sight.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: How much more is man abominable, and unprofitable, who drinketh iniquity like water?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I will shew thee, hear me: and I will tell thee what I have seen.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Wise men confess and hide not their fathers.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: To whom alone the earth was given, and no stranger hath passed among them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The wicked man is proud all his days, and the number of the years of his tyranny is uncertain.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The sound of dread is always in his ears: and when there is peace, he always suspecteth treason.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He believeth not that he may return from darkness to light, looking round about for the sword on every side.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: When he moveth himself to seek bread, he knoweth that the day of darkness is ready at his hand.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Tribulation shall terrify him, and distress shall surround him, as a king that is prepared for the battle.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For he hath stretched out his hand against God, and hath strengthened himself against the Almighty.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He hath run against him with his neck raised up, and is armed with a fat neck.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Fatness hath covered his face, and the fat hangeth down on his sides.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He hath dwelt in desolate cities, and in desert houses that are reduced into heaps.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He shall not be enriched, neither shall his substance continue, neither shall he push his root in the earth.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He shall not depart out of darkness: the flame shall dry up his branches, and he shall be taken away by the breath of his own mouth.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He shall not believe, being vainly deceived by error, that he may be redeemed with any price.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Before his days be full he shall perish: and his hands shall wither away.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He shall be blasted as a vine when its grapes are in the first flower, and as an olive tree that casteth its flower.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For the congregation of the hypocrite is barren, and fire shall devour their tabernacles, who love to take bribes.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He hath conceived sorrow, and hath brought forth iniquity, and his womb prepareth deceits.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then Job answered, and said:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I have often heard such things as these: you are all troublesome comforters.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Shall windy words have no end? or is it any trouble to thee to speak?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I also could speak like you: and would God your soul were for my soul.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I would comfort you also with words, and would wag my head over you.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I would strengthen you with my mouth, and would move my lips, as sparing you.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But what shall I do? If I speak, my pain will not rest: and if I hold my peace, it will not depart from me.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But now my sorrow hath oppressed me, and all my limbs are brought to nothing.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: My wrinkles bear witness against me, and a false speaker riseth up against my face, contradicting me.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He hath gathered together his fury against me, and threatening me he hath gnashed with his teeth upon me: my enemy hath beheld me with terrible eyes.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: They have opened their mouths upon me, and reproaching me they have struck me on the cheek, they are filled with my pains.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: God hath shut me up with the unjust man, and hath delivered me into the hands of the wicked.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I that was formerly so wealthy, am all on a sudden broken to pieces: he hath taken me by my neck, he hath broken me, and hath set me up to be his mark.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He hath compassed me round about with his lances, he hath wounded my loins, he hath not spared, and hath poured out my bowels on the earth,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He hath torn me with wound upon wound, he hath rushed in upon me like a giant.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I have sowed sackcloth upon my skin, and have covered my flesh with ashes.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: My face is swollen with weeping, and my eyelids are dim.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: These things have I suffered without the iniquity of my hand, when I offered pure prayers to God.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: O earth, cover not thou my blood, neither let my cry find a hiding place in thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For behold my witness is in heaven, and he that knoweth my conscience is on high.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: My friends are full of words: my eye poureth out tears to God.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And O that a man might so be judged with God, as the son of man is judged with his companion!

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For behold short years pass away, and I am walking in a path by which I shall not return.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: My spirit shall be wasted, my days shall be shortened and only the grave remaineth for me.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I have not sinned, and my eye abideth in bitterness.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Deliver me, O Lord, and set me beside thee, and let any man’s hand fight against me.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Thou hast set their heart far from understanding, therefore they shall not be exalted.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He promiseth a prey to his companions, and the eyes of his children shall fail.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He hath made me as it were a byword of the people, and I am an example before them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: My eye is dim through indignation, and my limbs are brought as it were to nothing.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The just shall be astonished at this, and the innocent shall be raised up against the hypocrite.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the just man shall hold on his way, and he that hath clean hands shall be stronger and stronger.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Wherefore be you all converted, and come, and I shall not find among you any wise man.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: My days have passed away, my thoughts are dissipated, tormenting my heart.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: They have turned night into day, and after darkness I hope for light again.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: If I wait hell is my house, and I have made my bed in darkness.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I have said to rottenness: Thou art my father; to worms, my mother and my sister.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Where is now then my expectation, and who considereth my patience?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: All that I have shall go down into the deepest pit: thinkest thou that there at least I shall have rest?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then Baldad the Suhite answered, and said:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: How long will you throw out words? understand first, and so let us speak.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Why are we reputed as beasts, and counted vile before you?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Thou that destroyest thy soul in thy fury, shall the earth be forsaken for thee, and shall rocks be removed out of their place?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Shall not the light of the wicked be extinguished, and the flame of his fire not shine?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The light shall be dark in his tabernacle, and the lamp that is over him, shall be put out.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The step of his strength shall be straitened, and his own counsel shall cast him down headlong.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For he hath thrust his feet into a net, and walketh in its meshes.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The sole of his foot shall be held in a snare, and thirst shall burn against him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A gin is hidden for him in the earth, and his trap upon the path.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Fears shall terrify him on every side, and shall entangle his feet.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Let his strength be wasted with famine, and let hunger invade his ribs.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Let it devour the beauty of his skin, let the firstborn death consume his arms.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Let his confidence be rooted out of his tabernacle, and let destruction tread upon him like a king.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Let the companions of him that is not, dwell in his tabernacle, let brimstone be sprinkled in his tent.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner
