text: Look up to heaven and see, and behold the sky, that it is higher than thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: If thou sin, what shalt thou hurt him? and if thy iniquities be multiplied, what shalt thou do against him?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And if thou do justly, what shalt thou give him, or what shall he receive of thy hand?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Thy wickedness may hurt a man that is like thee: and thy justice may help the son of man.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: By reason of the multitude of oppressors they shall cry out: and shall wail for the violence of the arm of tyrants.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he hath not said: Where is God, who made me, who hath given songs in the night?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Who teacheth us more than the beasts of the earth, and instructeth us more than the fowls of the air.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: There shall they cry, and he will not hear, because of the pride of evil men.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: God therefore will not hear in vain, and the Almighty will look into the causes of every one.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Yea, when thou shalt say: He considereth not: be judged before him, and expect him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For he doth not now bring on his fury, neither doth he revenge wickedness exceedingly.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Therefore Job openeth his mouth in vain, and multiplieth words without knowledge.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Eliu also proceeded, and said:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Suffer me a little, and I will shew thee: for I have yet somewhat to speak in God’s behalf.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I will repeat my knowledge from the beginning, and I will prove my Maker just.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For indeed my words are without a lie, and perfect knowledge shall be proved to thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: God doth not cast away the mighty, whereas he himself also is mighty.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But he saveth not the wicked, and he giveth judgment to the poor.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He will not take away his eyes from the just, and he placeth kings on the throne for ever, and they are exalted.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And if they shall be in chains, and be bound with the cords of poverty:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He shall shew them their works, and their wicked deeds, because they have been violent.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He also shall open their ear, to correct them: and shall speak, that they may return from iniquity.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: If they shall hear and observe, they shall accomplish their days in good, and their years in glory.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But if they hear not, they shall pass by the sword, and shall be consumed in folly.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Dissemblers and crafty men prove the wrath of God, neither shall they cry when they are bound.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Their soul shall die in a storm, and their life among the effeminate.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He shall deliver the poor out of his distress, and shall open his ear in affliction.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Therefore he shall set thee at large out of the narrow mouth, and which hath no foundation under it: and the rest of thy table shall be full of fatness.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Thy cause hath been judged as that of the wicked, cause and judgment thou shalt recover.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Therefore let not anger overcome thee to oppress any man: neither let multitude of gifts turn thee aside.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Lay down thy greatness without tribulation, and all the mighty of strength.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Prolong not the night that people may come up for them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Beware thou turn not aside to iniquity: for this thou hast begun to follow after misery.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Behold, God is high in his strength, and none is like him among the lawgivers.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Who can search out his ways? or who can say to him: Thou hast wrought iniquity?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Remember that thou knowest not his work, concerning which men have sung.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: All men see him, every one beholdeth afar off.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Behold, God is great, exceeding our knowledge: the number of his years is inestimable.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He lifteth up the drops of rain, and poureth out showers like floods:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Which flow from the clouds that cover all above.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: If he will spread out clouds as his tent,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And lighten with his light from above, he shall cover also the ends of the sea.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For by these he judgeth people, and giveth food to many mortals.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: In his hands he hideth the light, and commandeth it to come again.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He sheweth his friend concerning it, that it is his possession, and that he may come up to it.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: At this my heart trembleth, and is moved out of its place.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Hear ye attentively the terror of his voice, and the sound that cometh out of his mouth.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He beholdeth under all the heavens, and his light is upon the ends of the earth.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: After it a noise shall roar, he shall thunder with the voice of his majesty, and shall not be found out, when his voice shall be heard.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: God shall thunder wonderfully with his voice, he that doth great and unsearchable things.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He commandeth the snow to go down upon the earth, and the winter rain, and the shower of his strength.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He sealeth up the hand of all men, that every one may know his works.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then the beast shall go into his covert, and shall abide in his den.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Out of the inner parts shall a tempest come, and cold out of the north.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: When God bloweth there cometh frost, and again the waters are poured out abundantly.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Corn desireth clouds, and the clouds spread their light:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Which go round about, whithersoever the will of him that governeth them shall lead them, to whatsoever he shall command them upon the face of the whole earth:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Whether in one tribe, or in his own land, or in what place soever of his mercy he shall command them to be found.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Hearken to these things, Job: Stand, and consider the wondrous works of God.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Dost thou know when God commanded the rains, to shew his light of his clouds?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Knowest thou the great paths of the clouds, and the perfect knowledges?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Are not thy garments hot, when the south wind blows upon the earth?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Thou perhaps hast made the heavens with him, which are most strong, as if they were of molten brass.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Shew us what we may say to him: for we are wrapped up in darkness.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Who shall tell him the things I speak? even if a man shall speak, he shall be swallowed up.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But now they see not the light: the air on a sudden shall be thickened into clouds, and the wind shall pass and drive them away.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Cold cometh out of the north, and to God praise with fear.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: We cannot find him worthily: he is great in strength, and in judgment, and in justice, and he is ineffable.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Therefore men shall fear him, and all that seem to themselves to be wise, shall not dare to behold him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then the Lord answered Job out of a whirlwind, and said:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Who is this that wrappeth up sentences in unskilful words?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Gird up thy loins like a man: I will ask thee, and answer thou me.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Where wast thou when I laid the foundations of the earth? tell me if thou hast understanding.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Who hath laid the measures thereof, if thou knowest or who hath stretched the line upon it?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Upon what are its bases grounded? or who laid the corner stone thereof,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: When the morning stars praised me together, and all the sons of God made a joyful melody?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Who shut up the sea with doors, when it broke forth as issuing out of the womb:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: When I made a cloud the garment thereof, and wrapped it in a mist as in swaddling bands?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I set my bounds around it, and made it bars and doors:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And I said: Hitherto thou shalt come, and shalt go no further, and here thou shalt break thy swelling waves.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Didst thou since thy birth command the morning, and shew the dawning of the day its place?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And didst thou hold the extremities of the earth shaking them, and hast thou shaken the ungodly out of it?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The seal shall be restored as clay, and shall stand as a garment.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: From the wicked their light shall be taken away, and the high arm shall be broken.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Hast thou entered into the depths of the sea, and walked in the lowest parts of the deep?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Have the gates of death been opened to thee, and hast thou seen the darksome doors?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Hast thou considered the breadth of the earth? tell me, if thou knowest all things?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Where is the way where light dwelleth, and where is the place of darkness?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: That thou mayst bring every thing to its own bounds, and understand the paths of the house thereof.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Didst thou know then that thou shouldst be born? and didst thou know the number of thy days?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Hast thou entered into the storehouses of the snow, or hast thou beheld the treasures of the hail:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Which I have prepared for the time of the enemy, against the day of battle and war?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: By what way is the light spread, and heat divided upon the earth?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Who gave a course to violent showers, or a way for noisy thunder:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: That it should rain on the earth without man in the wilderness, where no mortal dwelleth:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: That it should fill the desert and desolate land, and should bring forth green grass?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Who is the father of rain? or who begot the drops of dew?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Out of whose womb came the ice? and the frost from heaven who hath gendered it?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The waters are hardened like a stone, and the surface of the deep is congealed.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Shalt thou be able to join together the shining stars the Pleiades, or canst thou stop the turning about of Arcturus?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Canst thou bring forth the day star in its time, and make the evening star to rise upon the children of the earth?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Dost thou know the order of heaven, and canst thou set down the reason thereof on the earth?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Canst thou lift up thy voice to the clouds, that an abundance of waters may cover thee?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Canst thou send lightnings, and will they go, and will they return and say to thee: Here we are?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Who hath put wisdom in the heart of man? or who gave the cock understanding?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Who can declare the order of the heavens, or who can make the harmony of heaven to sleep?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: When was the dust poured on the earth, and the clods fastened together?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Wilt thou take the prey for the lioness, and satisfy the appetite of her whelps,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: When they couch in the dens and lie in wait in holes?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Who provideth food for the raven, when her young ones cry to God, wandering about, because they have no meat?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Knowest thou the time when the wild goats bring forth among the rocks, or hast thou observed the hinds when they fawn?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Hast thou numbered the months of their conceiving, or knowest thou the time when they bring forth?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: They bow themselves to bring forth young, and they cast them, and send forth roarings.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Their young are weaned and go to feed: they go forth, and return not to them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Who hath sent out the wild ass free, and who hath loosed his bonds?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: To whom I have given a house in the wilderness, and his dwellings in the barren land.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He scorneth the multitude of the city, he heareth not the cry of the driver.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He looketh round about the mountains of his pasture, and seeketh for every green thing,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Shall the rhinoceros be willing to serve thee, or will he stay at thy crib?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Canst thou bind the rhinoceros with thy thong to plough, or will he break the clods of the valleys after thee?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Wilt thou have confidence in his great strength, and leave thy labours to him?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Wilt thou trust him that he will render thee the seed, and gather it into thy barnfloor?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The wing of the ostrich is like the wings of the heron, and of the hawk.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: When she leaveth her eggs on the earth, thou perhaps wilt warm them in the dust.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: She forgetteth that the foot may tread upon them, or that the beasts of the field may break them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: She is hardened against her young ones, as though they were not hers, she hath laboured in vain, no fear constraining her.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For God hath deprived her of wisdom, neither hath he given her understanding.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: When time shall be, she setteth up her wings on high: she scorneth the horse and his rider.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Wilt thou give strength to the horse or clothe his neck with neighing?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Wilt thou lift him up like the locusts? the glory of his nostrils is terror.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He breaketh up the earth with his hoof, he pranceth boldly, he goeth forward to meet armed men.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He despiseth fear, he turneth not his back to the sword.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Above him shall the quiver rattle, the spear and shield shall glitter.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Chasing and raging he swalloweth the ground, neither doth he make account when the noise of the trumpet soundeth.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: When he heareth the trumpet he saith: Ha, ha: he smelleth the battle afar off, the encouraging of the captains, and the shouting of the army.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Doth the hawk wax feathered by thy wisdom, spreading her wings to the south?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Will the eagle mount up at thy command, and make her nest in high places?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: She abideth among the rocks, and dwelleth among cragged flints, and stony hills, where there is no access.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: From thence she looketh for the prey, and her eyes behold afar off.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Her young ones shall suck up blood: and wheresoever the carcass shall be, she is immediately there.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the Lord went on, and said to Job:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Shall he that contendeth with God be so easily silenced? surely he that reproveth God, ought to answer him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then Job answered the Lord, and said:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: What can I answer, who hath spoken inconsiderately? I will lay my hand upon my mouth.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: One thing I have spoken, which I wish I had not said: and another, to which I will add no more.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the Lord answering Job out of the whirlwind, said:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Gird up thy loins like a man: I will ask thee, and do thou tell me.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Wilt thou make void my judgment: and condemn me, that thou mayst be justified?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And hast thou an arm like God, and canst thou thunder with a voice like him?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Clothe thyself with beauty, and set thyself up on high, and be glorious, and put on goodly garments.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Scatter the proud in thy indignation, and behold every arrogant man, and humble him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Look on all that are proud, and confound them, and crush the wicked in their place,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Hide them in the dust together, and plunge their faces into the pit.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then I will confess that thy right hand is able to save thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Behold behemoth whom I made with thee, he eateth grass like an ox.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: His strength is in his loins, and his force in the navel of his belly.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He setteth up his tail like a cedar, the sinews of his testicles are wrapped together.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: His bones are like pipes of brass, his gristle like plates of iron.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He is the beginning of the ways of God, who made him, he will apply his sword.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: To him the mountains bring forth grass: there all the beasts of the field shall play.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He sleepeth under the shadow, in the covert of the reed, and in moist places.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The shades cover his shadow, the willows of the brook shall compass him about.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Behold, he will drink up a river, and not wonder: and he trusteth that the Jordan may run into his mouth.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: In his eyes as with a hook he shall take him, and bore through his nostrils with stakes.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Canst thou draw out the leviathan with a hook, or canst thou tie his tongue with a cord?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Canst thou put a ring in his nose, or bore through his jaw with a buckle?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Will he make many supplications to thee, or speak soft words to thee?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Will he make a covenant with thee, and wilt thou take him to be a servant for ever,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Shalt thou play with him as with a bird, or tie him up for thy handmaids?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Shall friends cut him in pieces, shall merchants divide him?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Wilt thou fill nets with his skin, and the cabins of fishes with his head?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Lay thy hand upon him: remember the battle, and speak no more.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Behold his hope shall fail him, and in the sight of all he shall be cast down.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I will not stir him up, like one that is cruel, for who can resist my countenance?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Who hath given me before that I should repay him? All things that are under heaven are mine.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I will not spare him, nor his mighty words, and framed to make supplication.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Who can discover the face of his garment? or who can go into the midst of his mouth?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Who can open the doors of his face? his teeth are terrible round about.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: His body is like molten shields, shut close up with scales pressing upon one another.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: One is joined to another, and not so much as any air can come between them:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: They stick one to another and they hold one another fast, and shall not be separated.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: His sneezing is like the shining of fire, and his eyes like the eyelids of the morning.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Out of his mouth go forth lamps, like torches of lighted fire.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Out of his nostrils goeth smoke, like that of a pot heated and boiling.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: His breath kindleth coals, and a flame cometh forth out of his mouth.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: In his neck strength shall dwell, and want goeth before his face.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The members of his flesh cleave one to another: he shall send lightnings against him, and they shall not be carried to another place.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: His heart shall be as hard as a stone, and as firm as a smith’s anvil,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: When he shall raise him up, the angels shall fear, and being affrighted shall purify themselves.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: When a sword shall lay at him, it shall not be able to hold, nor a spear, nor a breastplate.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For he shall esteem iron as straw, and brass as rotten wood.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The archer shall not put him to flight, the stones of the sling are to him like stubble.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: As stubble will he esteem the hammer, and he will laugh him to scorn who shaketh the spear.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The beams of the sun shall be under him, and he shall strew gold under him like mire.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He shall make the deep sea to boil like a pot, and shall make it as when ointments boil.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A path shall shine after him, he shall esteem the deep as growing old.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: There is no power upon earth that can be compared with him who was made to fear no one,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He beholdeth every high thing, he is king over all the children of pride.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then Job answered the Lord, and said:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I know that thou canst do all things, and no thought is hid from thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Who is this that hideth counsel without knowledge? Therefore I have spoken unwisely, and things that above measure exceeded my knowledge.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Hear, and I will speak: I will ask thee, and do thou tell me.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: With the hearing of the ear, I have heard thee, but now my eye seeth thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Therefore I reprehend myself, and do penance in dust and ashes.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And after the Lord had spoken these words to Job, he said to Eliphaz the Themanite: My wrath is kindled against thee, and against thy two friends, because you have not spoken the thing that is right before me, as my servant Job hath.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Take unto you therefore seven oxen and seven rams, and go to my servant Job, and offer for yourselves a holocaust, and my servant Job shall pray for you: his face I will accept, that folly be not imputed to you: for you have not spoken right things before me, as my servant Job hath.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So Eliphaz the Themanite, and Baldad the Suhite, and Sophar the Naamathite went, and did as the Lord had spoken to them, and the Lord accepted the face of Job.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The Lord also was turned at the penance of Job, when he prayed for his friends. And the Lord gave Job twice as much as he had before.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And all his brethren came to him, and all his sisters, and all that knew him before, and they ate bread with him in his house: and bemoaned him, and comforted him upon all the evil that God had brought upon him. And every man gave him one ewe, and one earring of gold.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the Lord blessed the latter end of Job more than his beginning. And he had fourteen thousand sheep, and six thousand camels, and a thousand yoke of oxen, and a thousand she asses.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he had seven sons, and three daughters.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he called the name of one Dies, and the name of the second Cassia, and the name of the third Cornustibii.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And there were not found in all the earth women so beautiful as the daughters of Job: and their father gave them inheritance among their brethren.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Job lived after these things, a hundred and forty years, and he saw his children, and his children’s children, unto the fourth generation, and he died an old man, and full of days.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner
